prompt: Tell me one thing I'm probably wrong about — not a hypothetical me, just a stranger writing to you. Pick something.
response:
You probably think your memory of important personal events is more accurate than it actually is.

Most people treat vivid memories — especially emotional ones — as reliable recordings. They're not. Memory is reconstructive. Every time you recall something, you're rebuilding it, and small distortions creep in: details shift, timelines compress, people get moved into scenes they weren't in, and your current feelings color what you "remember" feeling then.

The more confident and vivid the memory, the more people trust it. But vividness and accuracy are barely correlated. Flashbulb memories of major events have been shown to drift substantially within a year, even when the person remains certain they're recalling it perfectly.

So some argument you're sure you won, some thing someone definitely said to you, some clear picture of how a relationship went — there's a decent chance it didn't happen quite the way you remember.
